clinical_coherence.notes: 32F East Asian with migraines with aura, iron deficiency anemia, anxiety, on combined OCP. Presents with acute expressive aphasia and right hemiparesis triggered during hyperventilation at work. Prior TIAs dismissed as panic attacks. Vitals show sinus tachycardia but otherwise normal. Neuro exam shows left MCA territory findings. Clinically coherent and compelling.

tool_output_consistency.notes: Excellent concordance. MRI shows acute left frontal opercular infarct with bilateral chronic watershed infarcts and prominent basal ganglia flow voids (moyamoya hallmarks). MRA shows bilateral ICA terminal stenosis. CTA confirms moyamoya collaterals. Normal echo with negative bubble study excludes PFO. Negative hypercoagulability panel. CT perfusion shows bilateral hypoperfusion with exhausted cerebrovascular reserve and steal phenomenon. All perfectly support moyamoya.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD I67.5 (moyamoya disease) is the correct specific code. Diagnosis accurately identifies moyamoya as the etiology. Critical actions are superb: avoid IV tPA in collateral-dependent circulation, discontinue OCPs, neurosurgical consultation for revascularization, screen family members, avoid hyperventilation/hypotension. Differential excludes PFO, intracranial atherosclerosis, and vasculitis. Red herrings well-documented.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Perfectly calibrated. Young East Asian woman with recurrent TIAs misdiagnosed as panic attacks, OCP use as a misleading risk factor, childhood migraines potentially representing early moyamoya symptoms. Requires recognizing bilateral watershed infarcts and moyamoya collaterals on imaging. Multiple red herrings. Expected agent confidence 0.40 appropriate.

demographic_plausibility.notes: Outstanding. 32F Korean-American -- East Asian ethnicity has the highest worldwide moyamoya prevalence. Mother with intracranial hemorrhage at 45 in Korea is a critical clue suggesting familial moyamoya. The history of childhood migraines with aura potentially representing early moyamoya symptoms is sophisticated and realistic.

red_herring_quality.notes: Three excellent red herrings: (1) OCP use suggesting hormonal cause -- actually a contributing factor but not the primary etiology; (2) Anxiety disorder with TIAs attributed to panic attacks -- a common real-world diagnostic delay; (3) Childhood migraines with aura suggesting migrainous infarction -- may actually be early moyamoya ischemia. All are realistic, well-documented, and genuinely misleading.

educational_value.notes: Exceptional. Teaches moyamoya disease recognition, hyperventilation-triggered TIAs as pathognomonic, bilateral watershed infarcts as hemodynamic signature, importance of family history in East Asian populations, cerebrovascular reserve assessment, and the critical management differences (no tPA, revascularization needed, stop OCPs). Multiple high-yield pearls including RNF213 gene variant.

strengths: Best-in-class diagnostic puzzle with genuine diagnostic challenge
Outstanding red herrings that realistically mislead
Family history as a critical diagnostic clue
Multiple pathognomonic features elegantly woven into the narrative
